text:
GENITOURINARY MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
OFFICE VISIT — ESTABLISHED PATIENT
ATTENDING: [GU Oncology Attending]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mrs. Margaret Okonkwo is a 63-year-old West African-descent woman with metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC), IMDC intermediate-risk, with 20-25% sarcomatoid differentiation, presenting today for review of first restaging imaging and transition from ipilimumab/nivolumab induction to nivolumab maintenance therapy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mrs. Okonkwo was originally diagnosed with Stage IV (cT3bN2M1) clear cell RCC of the right kidney approximately five months ago. Her presentation included an 11.4 cm right renal mass with IVC thrombus extension (infrarenal, non-occlusive), bulky retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy with multiple involved nodes in the aortocaval and para-aortic regions, and bilateral pulmonary metastases (four lesions, largest 1.9 cm in the right lower lobe). Sarcomatoid differentiation was identified in approximately 20-22% of the sampled tumor. PD-L1 by 22C3 assay was markedly elevated with a CPS of approximately 55. Comprehensive genomic profiling demonstrated VHL and PBRM1 as co-drivers, with additional SETD2 frameshift and CDKN2A deletion. TMB was intermediate-low at 6.2 mut/Mb; MSS.

Following multidisciplinary tumor board review, she underwent cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with RPLND and IVC thrombectomy. Surgical pathology confirmed pT3bN2 ccRCC with 9 of 14 lymph nodes involved, extranodal extension in the largest node (3.1 cm), and IVC thrombus measuring 4.2 cm with clear IVC wall margins. The sarcomatoid component was present in the lymph node metastases as well.

She initiated first-line combination immunotherapy with ipilimumab (ipi) 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks plus nivolumab (nivo) 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks approximately 8 weeks post-operatively, following a baseline restaging CT that showed four bilateral pulmonary nodules as the sole sites of evaluable disease (sum of diameters 5.0 cm, per baseline established on that post-op scan).

Treatment course summary:
- Cycle 1 (ipi/nivo): Tolerated well. Grade 1 fatigue, grade 1 nausea managed with PRN ondansetron, grade 1 loose stools.
- Cycle 2 (ipi/nivo): Administered without dose modification. Ongoing grade 1 GI symptoms and fatigue. TSH normal at cycle 2 check.
- Cycle 3 (ipi/nivo): Complicated by immune-related colitis, CTCAE Grade 2 — she developed 6-8 watery stools per day with lower abdominal cramping. Colonoscopy confirmed immune-mediated colitis with increased intraepithelial lymphocytes and crypt distortion on biopsy. Ipilimumab was held for one cycle; nivolumab was continued with caution. She was started on oral budesonide 9 mg daily. Symptoms improved to grade 1 within approximately 10 days. She was negative for infectious causes on stool cultures.
- Cycle 4 (ipi/nivo, administered with a 1-cycle delay): Ipilimumab was reintroduced after colitis resolved to grade 1 or less. She completed the fourth and final ipilimumab dose.

First restaging CT (performed approximately 12 weeks after starting ipi/nivo, i.e., approximately 3 months into therapy) showed a very encouraging partial response: all four pulmonary nodules shrank substantially. The right lower lobe lesion went from 2.1 cm to 1.3 cm, the right upper lobe from 1.2 cm to 0.7 cm, the left lower lobe from 0.9 cm to 0.5 cm, and the left upper lobe from 0.7 cm to 0.4 cm. The combined sum of the four target lesions dropped from 5.0 cm at baseline to approximately 2.9 cm — a reduction of roughly 42%. Retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy was not identified on restaging. No new sites of disease.

She presents today to review the scan results and formally transition to nivolumab 480 mg flat-dose IV every 4 weeks (maintenance, per CheckMate 214 protocol).

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
- Constitutional: Ongoing mild fatigue, which she describes as noticeably improved compared to the period immediately after surgery and early cycles. Reports feeling more energetic than she has in months. No fever, no chills, no night sweats.
- Gastrointestinal: Diarrhea has improved considerably from the peak grade 2 episode. Currently on budesonide taper and reporting 1-2 loose stools per day above her usual baseline. No blood in stool. No nausea at present (not needing ondansetron routinely). Appetite is good.
- Endocrine: No symptoms of thyroid dysfunction at this visit (no palpitations, heat or cold intolerance, tremor). TSH mildly decreased at this check (see labs). Will monitor.
- Pulmonary: No cough, no hemoptysis, no dyspnea. She comments that she is breathing more comfortably than before surgery.
- Musculoskeletal: No arthralgias, no myalgias, no joint swelling.
- Dermatologic: No rash, no pruritus.
- Neurologic: No headache, no visual changes, no focal deficits.
- Genitourinary: No dysuria, no hematuria. Voiding normally. On lisinopril was not yet started at this point (BP management not yet escalated).
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no leg edema.
- Psychological: Patient is emotionally relieved by the favorable scan results. She reports she has been anxious awaiting the imaging and is now cautiously optimistic. Continues to live with her adult daughter; family support described as strong.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital Signs: BP 136/86 mmHg, HR 74 bpm, RR 14, SpO2 97% on room air, weight 71.2 kg (up 1.8 kg from last visit, appropriate), temperature 36.8°C.
ECOG Performance Status: 1.

General: Alert, well-appearing West African woman in no acute distress. Appears more comfortable and less fatigued than on prior visits.
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric. No oral mucositis.
Neck: No thyromegaly. No cervical lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No peripheral edema.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, no wheeze.
Abdomen: Right flank well-healed surgical scar (laparotomy). Abdomen soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly. No palpable masses.
Extremities: No rash on palms or soles. No joint effusions. Distal pulses intact.
Neurologic: Alert and oriented x4. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal deficits.
Skin: No vitiligo, no maculopapular rash. No evidence of immune-related skin changes.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 6.8 x10^9/L (normal differential)
- Hemoglobin: 10.4 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia, stable; attributed to post-surgical/chronic disease state; no active bleeding)
- MCV: 88 fL
- Platelets: 224 x10^9/L

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L
- Potassium: 4.0 mEq/L
- BUN: 18 mg/dL
- Creatinine: 1.1 mg/dL (eGFR ~61 mL/min/1.73m2; acceptable for solitary kidney post-right nephrectomy)
- ALT: 38 U/L (normal)
- AST: 34 U/L (normal)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 88 U/L (normal)
- Total bilirubin: 0.5 mg/dL (normal)
- Albumin: 3.6 g/dL (low-normal)
- Glucose: 94 mg/dL

Endocrine:
- TSH: 0.4 mIU/L (mildly low; just below the lower limit of normal; free T4 not yet resulted at time of note — will review at next visit)
- AM cortisol: deferred (no clinical concern for adrenal insufficiency at this time; adrenal function was normal at baseline)

Urine dipstick: trace proteinuria (1+); baseline acceptable for solitary kidney.

Other:
- LDH: 198 U/L (normal upper limit ~240 at our institution)
- Lipase: 42 U/L (normal)

---

IMAGING REVIEW
The restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (performed approximately 12 weeks after starting ipi/nivo) was reviewed in detail at today's visit with the patient:

- Right lower lobe pulmonary nodule: down to 1.3 cm from 2.1 cm at the pre-therapy baseline — a substantial shrinkage of about 38%.
- Right upper lobe nodule: now 0.7 cm, down from 1.2 cm (approximately 42% smaller).
- Left lower lobe nodule: 0.5 cm, previously 0.9 cm (approximately 44% smaller).
- Left upper lobe nodule: 0.4 cm, previously 0.7 cm (approximately 43% smaller).
- Retroperitoneal region: no residual lymphadenopathy identified. The prior aortocaval and para-aortic nodes are no longer apparent, consistent with excellent nodal response.
- Liver, adrenals, bones: no metastatic disease. No new sites.

Overall, the four lung nodules all look meaningfully smaller — this is a clear and meaningful response to therapy. I reviewed the scans directly with Mrs. Okonkwo and her daughter, who were visibly relieved. I explained that while these lesions have shrunk significantly, small residual nodules likely still contain viable tumor cells and that we are not in a complete response situation — our goal now is to maintain this favorable control with ongoing nivolumab.

Prior MRI brain: negative for intracranial metastases (performed at staging). No neurologic symptoms to prompt repeat MRI at this time.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. METASTATIC CLEAR CELL RCC — PARTIAL RESPONSE ON FIRST-LINE IPILIMUMAB/NIVOLUMAB
   Mrs. Okonkwo has had an excellent response to ipi/nivo induction. All four evaluable pulmonary metastases shrank by approximately 38-44%, with no new disease and no residual retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy on restaging. This is consistent with a partial response and aligns with the favorable outcomes seen in the sarcomatoid subgroup in CheckMate 214, where ipi/nivo was associated with high response rates and particularly durable benefit in patients with sarcomatoid features and high PD-L1 expression — both of which apply to her.

   She has now completed the planned 4-dose ipilimumab induction (with a 1-cycle delay due to grade 2 colitis). Today she transitions to nivolumab monotherapy maintenance at 480 mg IV flat dose every 4 weeks.

   - Initiate nivolumab 480 mg IV q4 weeks starting today (or schedule for infusion this week per availability).
   - Pre-medications per institutional protocol (acetaminophen 650 mg PO and diphenhydramine 25 mg IV pre-infusion).
   - Next restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast in approximately 3 months (cycle 3 of maintenance, approximately 6 months total from therapy start). Will reassess response trajectory at that time.
   - Patient counseled that response durability on checkpoint inhibitor therapy is variable; some patients achieve prolonged disease control or even complete responses over time with continued nivolumab, while others may eventually experience progression. We will reassess goals of care and next steps based on ongoing imaging.

2. IMMUNE-RELATED COLITIS — RESOLVING, ON BUDESONIDE TAPER
   Grade 2 colitis developed during cycle 3 with ipilimumab; responded well to budesonide 9 mg daily. Current stool frequency 1-2 per day above baseline (grade 1 at most). She is now on a budesonide taper:
   - Continue budesonide 9 mg daily x 1 more week, then taper to 6 mg daily x 2 weeks, then 3 mg daily x 2 weeks, then discontinue.
   - Patient instructed to contact the clinic immediately if diarrhea worsens (>4 stools/day above baseline), if blood or mucus appears in stool, or if she develops significant abdominal pain.
   - No systemic corticosteroids currently needed; budesonide (minimally systemically absorbed) preferred to minimize immune suppression while she is starting nivolumab maintenance.
   - Monitoring: if colitis recurs on nivolumab monotherapy, may require permanent discontinuation and systemic corticosteroids with or without biologics (infliximab, vedolizumab).

3. MILDLY SUPPRESSED TSH — MONITORING
   TSH is 0.4 mIU/L today, borderline low. She is not on exogenous thyroid hormone supplementation at this point. This pattern can be seen early in immune checkpoint inhibitor-associated thyroiditis — a transient hyperthyroid phase before progression to hypothyroidism — or may represent mild subclinical fluctuation.
   - Order free T4 today (to be resulted); if free T4 is elevated, this would confirm early thyrotoxic phase of checkpoint inhibitor thyroiditis; if normal, reassure and monitor closely.
   - Repeat TSH and free T4 at next visit (approximately 4 weeks).
   - Instruct patient to report symptoms of thyrotoxicosis: palpitations, heat intolerance, tremor, excessive sweating, weight loss, anxiety.
   - No intervention today. Will adjust management based on free T4 result.

4. MILD ANEMIA
   Hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL, normocytic, stable from prior value of 10.8 g/dL at therapy initiation (and consistent with post-surgical anemia + anemia of chronic disease in setting of active malignancy). No signs of hemolysis (bili normal, LDH normal). No iron deficiency suggested (MCV 88 fL, no microcytosis).
   - No transfusion indicated at this time.
   - Monitor at each visit. Consider iron studies at next visit if anemia worsens.
   - Not attributing this to immune-mediated hemolytic anemia at this time.

5. HYPERTENSION — STABLE ON AMLODIPINE
   BP 136/86 mmHg today, acceptable on amlodipine 10 mg daily (baseline antihypertensive for her chronic hypertension). Blood pressure management will be an ongoing consideration, particularly as she continues immune checkpoint therapy and may require additional agents if TKI-class agents become relevant in the future.
   - Continue amlodipine 10 mg daily.
   - Reinforce low-sodium diet.

6. HYPERLIPIDEMIA
   Continuing home statin (not assessed today; deferred to PCP for ongoing management). No drug-drug interactions anticipated with nivolumab.

7. PERFORMANCE STATUS AND FUNCTIONAL STATUS
   ECOG PS 1. She reports meaningful improvement in energy and overall well-being compared to the peri-operative and early treatment period. She is walking daily, assisting with grandchildren, and managing her ADLs without difficulty. Palliative care co-management to be arranged formally to support goals of care and symptom management going forward.

8. GOALS OF CARE
   Brief discussion today given the favorable imaging results. Patient expresses that her priorities remain quality of life, maintaining her independence, and being able to be present and functional for her family. She understands the disease remains metastatic and incurable but is encouraged by the treatment response. She is not currently interested in discussing clinical trials but was advised she will be considered for appropriate trials should disease progression occur. Full palliative care integration will be arranged.

Return to clinic: in 4 weeks for nivolumab cycle 1 (maintenance dose 1) lab check and toxicity assessment, with formal office visit at 8 weeks. Restaging imaging in 12 weeks.

Patient verbalized understanding of the plan. Questions answered. She was accompanied by her adult daughter who was also engaged in the discussion. Written immunotherapy toxicity precautions reviewed and provided.

Time spent with patient: approximately 45 minutes, greater than 50% counseling and coordination of care.

[Electronically signed — GU Medical Oncology Attending]
